site.title: Statsbudget
site.description: An independent visualization of Sweden's public spending and state budget. Based on official data from Eurostat, ESV, SCB and the Riksdag.

hero.heading: Where your tax money goes
hero.subheading: See the full public sector at a glance and drill into the Swedish state budget from 1975 to today.
hero.sources: Sources: Eurostat · ESV · SCB · the Riksdag

explorer.noData: No data available for the selected year.
explorer.scopeNotePublic: Consolidated overview of Sweden's public spending using COFOG. This is the spending side, not only tax revenues. Click a function in the chart to see how it is distributed across central government, local government, and social security.
explorer.scopeNoteState: Detailed ESV view of the state budget. Click an expenditure area to see appropriations. Switch to Full public sector for the consolidated country-wide total.
explorer.budgetBalanceSource: Source: Ekonomifakta / ESV — state budget balance, billion SEK.
explorer.partyComparisonSource: Deviation from the government budget bill. Source: Riksdag budget motions.
explorer.noPartyData: No shadow budget data available for the selected year.

publicBreakdown.heading: Subsectors within {{area}}
publicBreakdown.note: The amounts show gross spending according to Eurostat and are therefore not directly comparable to the detailed state-budget data.

historical.heading: What the Swedish budget looked like 50 years ago
historical.intro: A look back at how the Swedish state allocated its spending during the 1970s and 1980s.
historical.placeholder: Editorial text on the political and economic context of this year will be added.

project.title: About the project
project.text: Statsbudget is a civic tool that makes both the Swedish state budget and the broader public sector more accessible and understandable. The project is run non-commercially with the goal of strengthening democratic transparency.

sources.title: Data sources
sources.esv: Ekonomistyrningsverket (ESV) — expenditure outcomes and appropriation levels
sources.riksdagen: the Riksdag — government bills and party budget motions

method.title: Method
method.text: Budget data is structured in a star schema with dimensions for year, expenditure area, appropriation, and party. Inflation adjustment uses the CPI index. Party shadow budgets are extracted from motions with AI assistance and sample-verified — errors may occur.

limitations.title: Limitations
limitations.text: Known limitations include: asymmetric granularity between outcomes and shadow budgets, historical sparsity before 2000, and the fact that party motions do not always specify appropriations at the same level of detail as the government proposal.

openData.title: Open data
openData.text: All data is available in our open GitHub repo:

reportError.title: Report an error
reportError.text: Found a mistake or have a question? Send us a report and we'll take a look:

skatteutgifter.title: Tax expenditures
skatteutgifter.intro: Tax expenditures are reductions in tax revenue that lower what the state collects. They are not appropriations in the budget — they live on the revenue side under inkomsttitel series 1700.
skatteutgifter.disclosure: ROT, RUT, mortgage interest deduction, ISK and the in-work tax credit are examples of tax expenditures. They reduce state tax revenue instead of appearing as appropriations on the spending side.
skatteutgifter.noData: No seeded numbers for the selected year. Run the seeder with a CSV exported from the government's tax expenditure annex to populate data.
skatteutgifter.caveat: Amounts come from the government's tax expenditure annex (bilaga to the budget bill). Some years are estimates rather than outcomes.
skatteutgifter.noMatch: No tax expenditures match your search.

comparison.heading: Who benefits? A comparison
comparison.intro: Many tax expenditures are available to everyone, but they disproportionately benefit those with high incomes or who own property and capital — the higher the income and more expensive the home, the larger the tax break in absolute terms. Here we compare these with means-tested benefits that disproportionately support low earners and the poor, as well as migration costs. Click a row to read more.
comparison.ratio: For every krona in means-tested benefits that disproportionately support low earners and the poor, roughly 3.5 kronor go to tax subsidies that disproportionately benefit high earners and the wealthy.
comparison.note: Tax expenditures and benefits are not directly comparable — tax expenditures reduce state revenue while benefits are actual spending. The comparison illustrates the order of magnitude.
comparison.dataNote: Amounts for means-tested benefits are estimates based on the latest available data from Socialstyrelsen, Pensionsmyndigheten, Försäkringskassan and the budget bill. Migration costs refer to expenditure areas 8 and 13 in the state budget.

rich.title: Tax expenditures that disproportionately benefit high earners and the wealthy

poor.title: Means-tested benefits that disproportionately support low earners and the poor

ranteavdrag.name: Mortgage interest deduction (households)
ranteavdrag.detail: 30% tax reduction on interest payments. The more expensive the home and higher the mortgage, the larger the deduction. A home mortgaged at 10M SEK yields ~150,000 SEK/year in tax relief — a renter gets nothing.

avkastningHem.name: Imputed return on owner-occupied homes
avkastningHem.detail: Living in your own home provides a tax-free "return" (you avoid paying rent to yourself). This benefit has been entirely untaxed since 2007, despite being counted as income in national accounts. More expensive home = larger invisible subsidy.

fastighetsskatt.name: Abolished property tax
fastighetsskatt.detail: The state property tax was replaced by a low capped municipal fee. The more expensive the home and the higher the assessed value, the larger the implicit tax discount compared with the old system.

tolvregler.name: Capital gains on qualified shares (3:12 rules)
tolvregler.detail: Owners of closely held companies can extract profits taxed at 20% capital gains instead of as salary with marginal tax up to ~55%. The National Audit Office has found these rules generous and primarily benefiting high earners.

rot.name: ROT deduction (home renovation)
rot.detail: 30% tax reduction on labor costs for home renovation, max 75,000 SEK/year. Requires homeownership and ability to afford renovations. Statistics Sweden data shows utilization rises sharply with income.

rut.name: RUT deduction (household services)
rut.detail: Tax reduction for household services (cleaning, childcare, etc.), max 75,000 SEK/year. Tax Agency statistics show that households earning over 600,000 SEK account for the majority of usage.

kapitalvinstBostad.name: Capital gains on home sales
kapitalvinstBostad.detail: Profit from selling a private home is taxed at 22% (via quota to 22/30) instead of the standard 30% capital rate. Larger home gains = more tax subsidy in absolute terms.

formogenhetsskatt.name: Abolished wealth tax
formogenhetsskatt.detail: The wealth tax was abolished in 2007. The gain accrues almost entirely to households with large financial assets and high net wealth.

avkastningBrf.name: Imputed return on co-op apartments
avkastningBrf.detail: Same principle as owner-occupied homes — co-op owners are not taxed on the implicit return of their housing. More expensive co-op = larger tax-free benefit.

miljobilar.name: Reduced benefit value for eco-friendly cars
miljobilar.detail: Company cars are taxed at a reduced benefit value if classified as eco-friendly. Company cars are most common among white-collar workers with high salaries, and the most expensive EVs (Tesla Model S/X, BMW iX) receive the largest reduction.

arvsskatt.name: Abolished inheritance tax
arvsskatt.detail: The inheritance tax was abolished in 2004. The benefit mainly arises for larger inheritances and capital transfers within already wealthy households.

uppskov.name: Deferred capital gains tax on homes
uppskov.detail: When switching homes, you can defer the tax on the gain. Effectively an interest-free loan from the state — most valuable for large gains (millions) in metropolitan areas.

onoterade.name: Capital gains on unlisted shares
onoterade.detail: Capital gains on non-qualified shares in unlisted companies are taxed at 25% instead of 30%. Only relevant for those with capital to invest in unlisted companies.

expertskatt.name: Expert tax relief
expertskatt.detail: Foreign experts, researchers and key employees can receive 25% of their income tax-free for up to seven years. It applies to high incomes and highly qualified roles.

ekonomisktBistand.name: Social assistance (welfare)
ekonomisktBistand.detail: The last safety net — fully means-tested subsistence support from the municipality. All other resources (savings, unemployment insurance, sick pay) must be exhausted first. 2024 standard for a single person: 4,180 SEK/month for personal costs.

bostadstillagg.name: Housing supplement for pensioners
bostadstillagg.detail: Means-tested supplement for low-income pensioners to cover housing costs. Reduced krona for krona with increased income. Max ~7,500 SEK/month.

aldreforsorjning.name: Elderly maintenance support
aldreforsorjning.detail: Basic security for pensioners with very low or no pension, e.g. people who immigrated late in life. Guarantees a minimum standard of living. Tested against all income.

bostadsbidrag.name: Housing allowance
bostadsbidrag.detail: Income-tested benefit for low-income families with children and young people (18–28) without children. Calculated based on income, housing cost and number of children. Phases out entirely at median income.

sjukAktivitet.name: Tax reduction for sickness and activity compensation
sjukAktivitet.detail: This tax reduction lowers the tax burden for people receiving sickness or activity compensation. It is clearly targeted at households with low or limited labour income and reduced work capacity.

etablering.name: Settlement benefit (new arrivals)
etablering.detail: Benefit during the first 2–3 years in the settlement program for newly arrived refugees. Conditional on participation in Swedish language courses, civic orientation and labor market programs. 308 SEK/day at full time.

underhallsstod.name: Child maintenance support
underhallsstod.detail: Försäkringskassan pays child maintenance when one parent fails to pay. Max 1,723 SEK/month per child. The state's cost is the net after recovery from the liable parent.

uo8.name: EA 8 — Migration
uo8.detail: Swedish Migration Agency operations: asylum processing, reception, detention, return, work permits and citizenship. Includes housing and daily allowance for asylum seekers.

uo13.name: EA 13 — Integration and equality
uo13.detail: Municipal reimbursements for refugee reception, settlement measures, civic orientation, interpretation services and home furnishing loans. Most of it is state reimbursement to municipalities and regions for actual costs.

skatteintakter.title: Tax revenues
skatteintakter.intro: Where does the government's money come from? This page shows how total tax revenues are distributed across different tax types and income categories, and how they have changed over time.
skatteintakter.disclosure: Data sourced from ESV (Swedish National Financial Management Authority) covering the state budget's revenue side. Some years are forecasts.
skatteintakter.noData: No data available for the selected year.
skatteintakter.noMatch: No income titles match your search.
skatteintakter.forecastCalloutBody: The figures for {{year}} are forecasts from the budget bill, not actual outcomes. The latest actual year is {{latestActualYear}}.
skatteintakter.flowNote: The total includes taxes collected by the state on behalf of municipalities and the pension system. This is why it exceeds the state budget's expenditures.
skatteintakter.caveat: Amounts refer to the state budget's revenue side. Municipal taxes are partly reported separately. Some years are forecasts, not actuals.

footer.tagline: An independent visualization of Sweden's public spending and state budget. Based on official data from Eurostat, ESV, SCB and the Riksdag.
footer.copyright: © {{year}} statsbudget.se — Data from ESV, SCB and the Riksdag
footer.disclaimer: The website is provided as-is. No responsibility is taken for the accuracy, completeness, or timeliness of the information. Official records at ESV, SCB and the Riksdag prevail.

banner.heading: Built with AI.
banner.body: Data is sourced from ESV, SCB and the Riksdag but has been processed and structured with AI assistance. Individual numbers may contain errors.

chat.title: Ask Statsbudget
chat.emptyState: Ask me anything about the Swedish state budget. I can look up figures for any expenditure area from 1975 to today.
suggestions: What did the most money go to in 2025?
How much do the wealthy get in tax deductions compared to what the poor get in benefits?
What has increased and decreased the most since 2015?
chat.error: Something went wrong. Please try again shortly.
chat.serverNotConfigured: The chat feature is not configured yet.
chat.sendHint: Enter to send · Shift+Enter for newline
